posts.cooked: Wow, I never would have thought that when I bought a few '05 SLBs to learn about Burgundy that I would have to wait 25-30 years to enjoy them.

My goodness there is a depth of fruit in this wine. Saturated and rich. Does it have the tannic backbone to go the distance? Maybe not but then I probably don’t either. I wonder if this will shut down. It wouldn’t shock me if it never does.

Pretty f’ing great stuff.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked:
Just got back from an extended vacation in Fl. Brought a bunch of wine but I’ll highlight the most interesting ones.

17 EMH Black Cat Cabernet - The word that comes to mind was delicious. Excellent body and well rounded. No green from the vintage. Drinking very well and all 4 of us commented on how good this wine is.

14 Martini Monte Rosso Zin - Had with the fried chicken at The Beachcomber. Again drinking very well. Zin is really an under appreciated restaurant wine and I will make sure to recommend it more. Didn’t have great length but I would certainly drink now.

55 L’Evangile - Drinking very well. Fruit was very fresh still and couldn’t detect any tannin. Finish was medium but stayed fresh till we finished.

06 DRC Echezeaux.- Really good. One of the best bottles of the year and certainly my favorite burgundy. After a couple of hours this really opened up. (I did have to swirl the decanter for much of that). Great nose of red fruits and spice. Finish was mouth coating. Loved this. Some of my favorite DRC’s have been the ECh.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Nick_Gangas:

55 L’Evangile - Drinking very well. Fruit was very fresh still and couldn’t detect any tannin. Finish was medium but stayed fresh till we finished.

Wow, amazing it showed so well! In blind tastings, L’Evangile continues to be among my favorites. I’ve purchased a few, but not ‘great’ vintages, per se…this note on the '55 gives me a bit of hope

posts.name: Brian Pinci
posts.cooked: 2015 Aubert CIX Chardonnay - I just went to the Chardonnay section of the cellar without really noticing what I actually pulled to drink. This was quite nice. Well balanced, with a floral nose and tropical fruit. Very long finish. I am surprised that TWA rated this 100. While it was very good and delicious, not sure I see it as a perfect wine.

Deep garnet color in the glass.

This wine was singing! On the nose, very complex. Leather and chocolate notes, with accents of black cherries and raspberries coming in, along with hints of brown spices and some minerality.

Incredibly balanced with a long finish. Dry with medium plus body and tannins with the right amount of acidity. Decanted for two hours before drinking.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
2015 Buondonno Lemme Lemme
This was one of a few bottles tasted on a 2017 visit to Chianti that we brought home with us. It was a pleasure for the memories, but more importantly for the wine itself. Absolutely delicious, smooth, full fruited, with a suggestion of balsamic for me and licorice for my wife. Excellent wine!

image1920×2560 441 KB

9/12/22 Added a little more info about this wine. (Translated from Italian on the Buondonno website). I was prompted by reading a recent BYOB post by @K_F_o_l_e_y where he mentioned Va La Vinyards Mahogany wine, an unknown to me wine with a field blend mystique that reminded me of the Lemme Lemme.

Produced from old vines married to maple planted from the 1930s to the 1950s, which survived the cultivation specialization because they are located near country roads and close to ancient buildings.
It is an “old Chianti” with a prevalence of Sangiovese but in which there are several other local varieties.

Toscana Rosso i.g.t.

Grapes: Sangiovese, Canaiolo, Malvasia Nera, Colorino and other varieties
Vineyard: old rows around Casavecchia and Sicellino
Soil: clayey-calcareous with abundant stony skeleton
Altitude and exposure: 430 mt. s.l.m .; NE, East
Average density: 500 plants per hectare
Training system: vines married to field maple (loppo, opium or testucchio)
Vinification: spontaneous fermentation with indigenous yeasts and part of the stalks for a period of about one month. Light pumping over and punching down for the entire vinification period
Aging: in 400 lt ceramic (Clayver) vats. for about a year
Production: average: 1,000 bottles

This thing is still youthful. Very dark opaque ruby, very viscous with long thick legs in the glass. Nose has tons of earth, cedar and a touch of herbal menthol notes and forest floor. Finish is very complex and long. I would run out of fingers, toes and likely hair if I counted how many seconds it lasts.
In my very uneducated opinion, this has recently reached close to it’s peak and will likely stay here for a long time. Good sh*t.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
On Saturday actually…
An overnight trip to Portland presented us with an unexpected bonus opportunity to meet and spend some time with @Dan_Kravitz over lunch at a BYOB Maine Lobster Shack. Dan arrived lugging a tote in each arm, like a travelling ‘drummer’ carrying the wares of his long-practiced trade. The wine glasses came out first, then four bottles of fine white wine for me to make a selection for lunch.

image1920×2560 309 KB

A 2013 Mt Eden Chardonnay was my choice and it took my Chardonnay drinking experience to a new level. Dark yellow, which Dan described as characteristic. Complex and fairly intense flavors with reminders of Chablis, minerals and herbs. Dan also noted subtle flavors of pine, and I immediately linked them to a finesse-ful IPA that I had enjoyed the night before. I kept drinking that Chardonnay, yet miraculously my wine glass never emptied until the bottle was gone thanks to Dan’s attentive eye.

image1920×2560 385 KB

Dan gifted us a bottle of his own 2013 Domaine Cabirau Maury Sec to take home with us.

Conversation about wine for sure, but also person to person moments that I especially appreciated. Good time. Thanks, Dan.

posts.name: Luis Manuel Debuque Monreal Ermitaño
posts.cooked:
Re: Domaine François Gaunoux’s Les Rugiens, Les Epenots & Grands Epenots, I’ve had different experiences with them in different vintages. Of those, off the top of my head, I’ve had ‘95, ‘97, ‘98, 99 & ‘00, though not of all 3 Pommards in each of said vintages. In my experience, ‘95 has been the least pleasing. The ‘98 Les Epenots/Grands Epenots need around 2-2&1/2 hours aeration in decanter (in my case while kept in my cav, given the Philippines’ tropical clime) to show well - without such aeration, the wine comes off as rather closed, flat, thin & uninteresting.

The ‘97 Les Rugiens & ‘99 Les Epenots, around 1-1&1/2 hours in decanter is enough. I’ve gone through around 8-10 bottles of the ‘97 Les Rugiens the past year and a half; and plan to buy more. The ‘98 I still have/buy/drink; but, usually, only when I don’t have any ‘97s & ‘99s on hand.

The one time I had their ‘00 Les Rugiens was in a (now defunct) restaurant in the old part of Beaune in early spring of 2013 - popped & poured, and it was already quite nice after around 30 minutes sitting in glass. That was quite a while back, though; so, I wouldn’t know how it shows/behaves now.

I stopped the install Friday, but two large panels are installed but not fully dried in. Could be ugly.

We had some friends over last night. Opened a 2015 Goodfellow Durant Chardonnay, a 2015 Eyrie Original Vines Chardonnay, and a 2006 Brick House Blackberry Port. The Goodfellow and Eyrie were both great. The Goodfellow was a little more reduced going on, but they both had great flavor and concentration. If anything, the Goodfellow was a tiny bit more intense. The 2006 Brick House Port was made from fruit harvested by the Etzel boys from the deer fence that surrounds the Brick House vineyards. It was rich, concentrated dark fruit flavors with an intense finish, though not terribly complex. This wine was not available for sale. I got my bottle as a Christmas gift over a decade ago.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
2008 Chateau Pontet-Canet

Disjointed and acidic as pop and pour. Left open for a day and the wine started to sing. Deep dark ruby with a slightly developed rim. Nose is somewhat developed and complex with powerful cassis and currants, old cedarwood and pencil shavings, touch of smoked cigars. Absolutely fantastic nose. Palate is medium intensity with cassis, leather, nice tingling acidity, slightly tart red fruit. Finish is long, intense, complex and long. I think this is still going uphill and can be enjoyed for at least 10+ years. Amazing wine.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
• 2019 Anne et Jean-Francois Ganevat De Toute Beauté - France, Vin de France (10/1/2022)
    As I understand it, The blend changes yearly, but consists of mostly Gamay from Morgon and Fleurie blended with other varieties. Some years indigenous Jura varieties, in others, the likes of Syrah from Cote Rotie and Mondeuse from Savoie are added. I couldn't find any definitive source for the blend in this vintage.

    Light in colour. Very open and expressive aromatically. Red fruits, cranberry, pomegranate, grilled watermelon. Worn saddle leather and a few floral notes. A slight effervescence on the palate at first, but that dies down with some 20 minutes in the glass. On the palate the fruit is bright, fleshy and slightly spicy (is that the Syrah or Mondeuse? Hard to say). Missing a smidge more acid to give the wine some lift and carry it through. As it stands, there's a disappointingly abrupt and short finish.

    ABV: 13.1%
    Closure: natural cork
    Stem: Grassl Cru
    Decant: 30min; drank over the course of ~4h

2009 J-F Ganevat Chalasses VV
Absolutely massive; so much power and resonance on the palate, and yet kept beautifully in shape by well-integrated fresh acidity that’s just a touch cidery on the finish to remind you that this is a natural wine; in my experience it’s rare to find Chardonnays with such monumental proportions that don’t feel forced or worked; I would have liked some additional aromatic detail to complement the main citrusy note, but that’s really splitting hairs; very good minus

2012 Grange des Peres
Very similar to the 2005 we had in Bordeaux but with a little extra help from the tannins, which lend some very welcome textural interest to balance the sweet/sour fruit juice sensations; would probably look to drink these even younger in the future as I think the structure dissipates far before the emergence of any interesting taste/smell nuances; very good minus, drink up

Belated birthday dinner at Rêve. Met expectations which is less common than one would expect for such restaurants in Singapore. Cooking was well-executed like a safe pair of hands, liked the chawanmushi and seafood dishes more than the meat ones. The dessert was simple but impressive too.

  • 2011 Château Magdelaine - France, Bordeaux, Libournais, St. Émilion Grand Cru (7/10/2022)
    After a tough couple of weeks, this was a perfect antidote. Incredibly perfumed and seductive nose with a slight tinge of greenness. Layers of ripe and pretty red fruit that had incredible depth and flavour, loved the elegance and classicism of this. Transcended the supposed weak vintage and reminded me why this is the only Bordeaux I collect as it is one of the very few Bordeaux to have that finesse and magic. (94 pts.)

  • 2018 Vouette et Sorbée Champagne Fidèle - France, Champagne (8/10/2022)
    100% PN that gave it a pale salmon colour which was unusual. Definitely a natty champagne but the style worked well. Not the most refined bubbles or fruit but the oxidative style and fresh, creamy strawberry fruit was very enjoyable with food. (92 pts.)
  • 2015 Aurélien Chatagnier St. Joseph - France, Rhône, Northern Rhône, St. Joseph (8/10/2022)
    Lovely perfume of blueberries with meaty undertones. Warm fruit but still unmistakably classic northern Rhone. Agree with previous notes that the tannins are a little drying but the fruit is vibrant enough to overlook that. (91 pts.)
  • 2015 Louis Jadot Côte de Nuits Villages Le Vaucrain - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Côte de Nuits Villages (8/10/2022)
    Had a muted nose. Palate was a little better with high acidity for a warm vintage. Plenty of earth and sour cherries but overall rather tart. Bit meh. (87 pts.)

posts.name: Y@0 C <aitch> 00 ng
posts.cooked:
We named four dogs (from the same litter) after some of our favorite wineries, and every year meet up to celebrate their birthdays

2014 Kenzo Estate Cabernet Sauvignon ‘Ai’
Decanted 2h prior; friendly and welcoming, with buxom reddish fruit that remains relatively bright (by Napa standards anyway); certainly a little simpler than the Harlan tasted alongside but that’s a high bar to clear; went down a treat with no complaints; good

2006 Harlan
Decanted 2h prior; darker and a bit broody, with earth/creosote that evokes at least an idea of Pauillac, though the scale and sweetness to the fruit makes it clear that this is not classic Bordeaux; structure is nicely judged, with palpable tannic grip that takes several more hours to finally release, and in full control of all the goodies on display; impressive, even if the value quotient might be a tiny bit lacking; very good minus

1989 Margaux
Decanted 1h prior; shows a lot of class, with these subtle delicious layers of fruit and earthy funk playing off of one another (no green); some thought it was a little dirty, and I could certainly see that; the most elegant of the four wines, and the one I missed the most when my glass was empty, even if the Harlan seems objectively the better wine; good plus

1997 Mouton
Decanted 1h prior, which was a mistake; the best glass was right after decanting, with that bright sweet plum on display that I consider the hallmark of Mouton combined with more than a little leafiness, and a lithe texture that went down a treat; both the fruit and body faded faster than I thought it would, and this was a pristinely stored bottle from release; drink up; good with a question mark

posts.name: S teve R edenbaugh
posts.cooked: Yao, for whatever reason the '01 seemed a bit reticent or shy on this evening. I recall seeing an early interview with Christian in which he said he wavered between the '91 and '94 as being his best California vintage. And i’ve met two other Dominus nuts who swear the '91 is it. I just speak from my own experience…I purchased a case of "94 on release and I swear its the best domestic wine I’ve had in 35 years of being in the wine trade…I’ve drank the wine on probably 20 or 25 occasions and it’s never let me down. Over the past 3 years I’ve purchased at least 2 more cases at auction…still a profound sample of new world Cabernet made to an old world standard. I think I need to search out some '91 and sample them side by side.

posts.name: Evan K0rn
posts.cooked:
image_671610891920×2560 330 KB

1934 Viuva Gomes Colares.

I visited the producer recently and mentioned I had this bottle – he said to drink up! With bottles like this, it’s sometimes hard to know when to pull the cork. I’m trying to stop waiting for the perfect occasion to open bottles and to let the bottles be an occasion in and of themselves… so a random Tuesday night it was.

Initially, the wine had quite a bit of funk to the nose and had very little to offer on the palate – the overriding note was iron. With time in a decanter and in our glasses, the nose gradually opened up to show a bit of shy, sweet red fruit behind a more dominant note of dead leaves (much like the smell of a walk through the woods around this time of year). The iron receded on the palate a bit with tart red fruit coming to the fore.

Much more alive than the 1969 I had last month. Quite an interesting wine to have in one’s glass (due to its age, the region it comes from and the grape), but perhaps more notable for it’s longevity at this point.

posts.name: Lasse Knudsen
posts.cooked:
20221108_1916581920×2560 236 KB

Moritz Kissinger, Chardonnay, 2019.

Moritz is yet another young and talented winemaker from Germany (Rheinhessen).

I have tasted his Null Ohm before. But this is a wine at another level. A wine with more depth and complexity.

It is mineral and lemon driven with a high acidic grip that adds real tension and freshness to the wine. Only 12,5% abv. Thats the frame. Then there are all the subtleties in the background. The slightly reductive note, the yellow fruits, some salt, fresh hay, butter and raw fennel with some herbs added.

I had it over three days and it kept improving. This is such a good and interesting wine that have some of the same qualities i look for in my beloved Jura Chardonnay.

This was my only bottle. Wish i had a full case in the cellar. This will age very well.

posts.name: E. Mark Larson
posts.cooked:
IMG_9668480×640 96.7 KB

IMG_9675480×640 114 KB

CellarTracker categorizes this wine as a “Red Blend.” but. as you can see from the back label, this 2019 bottling is a varietal wine made from the Paseante Noir grape. Paseante Noir is a hybridized grape that has been recently developed to be resistant to Pierce’s disease. I could not resist the opportunity to try this wine made from what is for me a new grape. Translucent ruby color. Rustic foliage on the nose. A quick hit of candied red fruit on the tip of the tongue, but that “candied” sensation seems to diminish quickly. Some mid-palate stoniness. Acid spritz on the edges of the tongue. Something of a fungal/mushroomy sensation under the rear palate. Not a terribly full-bodied wine, but, certainly, not a wimp. A pleasant wine. Obviously, I had no idea what to expect from this wine. If I were to try to peg it, I would say it is similar to a Zinfandel, although, I might also call out Syrah. I think it would be interesting to try one with a couple more years of bottle age.

2001 Torres Cabernet Sauvignon (Black Label) Mas La Plana

I like these random Winebid purchases that turn out to be winners. Especially when the most recent TNs in CT are over 5 years old.

Bright garnet with slight bricking. Initially has some balsamic VA that mostly blows away. Still somewhat youthful nose of blackcurrants, creme de cassis, tar, some menthol, touch of development with fig and mushrooms. Palate is mouth filling, full bodied and fresh with red fruits, cassis, some slightly tart cranberries and touch of leather. Finishes long with nice pleasant soft-ish tannins and more tar and leather. This is likely drinking close to peak right now. Very good stuff.

posts.name: John Glas
posts.cooked:
• 2014 Bergström Pinot Noir Cumberland Reserve - USA, Oregon, Willamette Valley
    Tasted blind. 2 glass over 3 hours. This was my 2nd least favorite wine and not a big fan of Bergstrom in general. So many better in Willamette. Earth, mushroom, violets and underbrush. Shorter finish and lacks personality. This for sure is drink now. (2 totals votes) (88 pts.)

  • 2013 Domaine Serene Pinot Noir Côte Sud Vineyard - USA, Oregon, Willamette Valley
    Tasted Blind. My second favorite wine of the night. Nice fruit featuring cherry and strawberry. Floral, flint, bramble, dust and earth. Medium plus finish and nice acidity. This has plenty left in the tank and should drink well through 2028. 93+ points (7 total points) (93 pts.)

  • 2015 Philippe Leclerc Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru Les Cazetiers - France, Burgundy, Côte de Nuits, Gevrey-Chambertin 1er Cru
    Tasted blind. My third favorite wine of the night. Rose petals, underbrush, cherry blossoms, wafter, spice and earth. Medium plus finish and nice acidity. Drink through 2030. (14 points) Tie for wine of the night. (92 pts.)

  • 2017 Ayres Vineyard Pinot Noir Lewis Rogers Lane Ribbon Ridge - USA, Oregon, Willamette Valley, Ribbon Ridge
    Tasted blind. Sour cherry, dust, minerals, spices and red currants. Good acidity and balanced wine. A solid effort and good value. (2 total points) (92 pts.)

  • 2019 Drew Family Cellars Pinot Noir Fog Eater - USA, California, North Coast, Anderson Valley
    Tasted blind. I have had these 2 other times and still did not know this was my wine. Volcanic ash, floral, mild funk, strawberry, earth and wild mushrooms. Super complex offering and the most exciting wines a the tasting. 4 first place votes out of six people. (14 points) Tied for wine of the night. (94 pts.)

  • 2018 Merry Edwards Pinot Noir Russian River Valley - USA, California, Sonoma County, Russian River Valley
    Tasted blind. Candied fruits, cranberry, floral and smoke. Just not the kind of Pinot I want to drink. Could use more acidity to balance out the wine. Really over-priced in my opinion. The only bottle with 1/4 fill left at the end of the night if that tells you anything. (1 point) (88 pts.)

  • 2010 Lieutenant de Sigalas - France, Bordeaux, Sauternais, Sauternes
    How someone has given this wine 94 and 96 is beyond me. Probably got this one mixed up with the other Sigalas. Semi sweet with apricots, honey suckles and light fruits. Lacks concentration and structure. Needs more acidity also. Weak effort. (85 pts.)

Stunning.

  • 1982 Château Haut-Brion - France, Bordeaux, Graves, Pessac-Léognan (11/15/2022)
    Decant off sediment. Cork was almost fully saturated which was worrysome, but there was good fruit on the nose. Seemed a bit tight so rested in the decanter for 2 hours then back in the bottle to the restaurant. Poured 2 hours later and absolutely singing on the nose, in a perfect place. Black and blue fruit, fresh tobacco, saddle leather, herbs on the nose. Luxurious mouthfeel, velvety, fully integrated tannins, fruit and spice with some charcuterie on the palate. Finish goes on and on and on. Followed over 2 hours and didn't really drop off, but didn't improve. I think we got it right in the sweet spot with the decant time. Extremely sexy in a masculine way. Like finding your favorite, worn leather gloves that fit perfectly and you thought you lost. (97 points)

posts.name: Gary Zimberg
posts.cooked:
All purchased at the wineries last year. While neither are producers I drink regularly both were surprisingly great. The Magniens with perfect provenance being a Library Release were especially surprising. The tour was very interesting and seeing their move to a more non intervention type of wine making was very interesting. Seeing Concrete Eggs and Amphorae being used on more recent releases raised my curiosity.

The 2000 Morey was almost like a Clos St Denis with its femininity, grace and length.
The St George was amazing - an intoxicating nose with such complexity followed by Nuit darker berry profile (yes with oak present but in balance)
Both wines show they still have plenty of time left - but are drinking really well right now.

We stopped into Bertagna only because we had finished a tour at Chateau La Tour and this was right next door and we walked by. I rare white Vougeot (Chardonnay) which had Pugliny Minerality and beautiful white fruits.

posts.cooked:
2005 Chateau Musar

Beautiful dark red with slightly evolved rim. Initially some lifted VA that blows away after a while. Complex somewhat developed nose of red cherries, strawberries, raisins, umami laden soy sauce, slightly green herbal notes. Palate is medium+ to full bodied, perfectly ripe with ample acidity and great balance, tart cherries, mushrooms, earthy minerality and some sweet red fruit. Finishes long, complex with still nice grip from chewy tannins. Yummy stuff. This is likely at its peak but will stay here for a long time. Glad I have few more bottles.

@Otto_Forsberg spoke highly of this vintage so I bought some. Very glad I did. The technical term I would use for this wine is “good sh*t”.

posts.name: Andrew Morgan
posts.cooked:
A compressed week in LN, UK, meeting rooms and pitches over. “Goblin mode” turned off. Back to family, Wales and the monthly (xmas) wine tasting

Ridge tasting tonight (non zin). A super strong showing. Benchmark wines I’d had my epiphany experiences as part of my early years in wine appreciation. Since which the prices have doubled. I always love the epiphany evenings but I find they belong to wines that one has less experience with.

These are superb wines. If they appear on a restaurant list at non eye watering prices I’m always tempted.

2008 smashed it in terms of the estate cab sav. The others I think you can do better from South Africa currently relative to cost, and that includes the Chardonnay. Au Bon Climat edges this chard for me (just about)

These wines are new world with Bordeaux (old world) aspirations. High altitude (relatively). Not huge ABV. Parker scored them lower which until relatively recently kept their prices at bay. No longer.

The labels are all super informational a product of the 4 Stanford uni founders and their academic discipline.

The divide between estate and montebello I’ll reach to Fourier analysis, image compression techniques or hifi parlance.

They share a lot of the same low frequency information, bass notes or principal components but the Montebello provides the additional detail, high frequency response. The subtle expressive stroke in the artistry. The nose, elusive and multi faceted

2011 montebello the consensus WOTN, a savoury note, Bordeaux clone. Indistinguishable between high end Bordeaux. Mine was the 2008 as it reflected the maturity I look for.

This wine tasting would have benefited from wines 10 years older too. They had hardly aged at all. Glistening pearly white teeth Californian smiles on the lot of them

The 2020 gets better over a few days and cellar for long term enjoyment.

  • 2020 Tensley Syrah Colson Canyon Vineyard - USA, California, Central Coast, Santa Barbara County (8/4/2022)
    Day 1: This is showing really well and for $32 a bottle hello! Exotic spices, black pepper, underbrush, blackberries, floral, earth and sour cherry. Medium plus and pleasing finish. For sure need to get more. 94 points
    Day 2: Sour berries, exotic spices, black pepper, floral, underbrush, earth and dust. Very balanced effort and nice acidity. This is a stellar bottle. 94 points
    Day 3: On day 1 and 2 each glass offered a sweet fruit taste. It always blew off as indicated with the notes above. Day 3 this is wine of the year territory especially at the price of $32. Wow! Black pepper, blueberries, boysenberries, bitter chocolate, floral, Asian spices, crushed rock and smoked meats. I had a lot of great Syrah wines from Walla Walla last month but this in a blind tasting might beat them all. 95+ points
    Recommendation: I normally don’t buy a case of any wine but making an exception here! Patients will reward you on this wine and it is not a pop and pour. (95 points)

posts.name: Andrew Demaree
posts.cooked:
Catching up on some pre-Christmas bottles shared with family.

image1920×2560 390 KB

2009 Larkmead Vineyards ‘LMV Salon’
Just a beautiful example of a Napa cab blend done in a truly balanced style, with the fruit well integrated with ample tannins and lifting acidity. The result is a seamless wine that impresses with its elegance as much as its depth of flavor. Subtle touches of menthol appear from time to time, which seem to be a hallmark of the vineyard. This is fully developed and ready to drink but in no danger of fading soon. I wish more Napa blends were made in this style.

2015 Realm Cellars ‘The Tempest’
Wow, this is a large-scale Merlot with a ton of depth and extraction. Rich, dark toned fruits are complemented by suggestions of coffee, black licorice, and asphalt. Full-bodied and mouth-filling, it demands your attention at each sip. There’s good structure framed by a wooly blanket of tannins lurking in the background. This is a super impressive Merlot in this style and likely has at least 6-7 years of prime drinking ahead of it.

image1920×2466 337 KB

2017 Mark Ryan ‘Olivia Grace’
We drank this GSM blend alongside a 2016 Chiron Grenache, and it was an interesting comparison. This is clearly the lighter bodied of the two, with a mix of sweet and sour cherries, leather, and tree bark on the palate. Cleanly made and svelte, this was a very nice food companion with our Christmas Eve dinner of pheasant cassoulet. Saved the last two glasses in the bottle for the next day to see how it evolved and it was lovely.

2016 Chiron ‘Aid and Succor’ Grenache
We consumed this alongside the 2017 Mark Ryan “Olivia Grace”. We liked both wines, but all of us had a preference for this one on the night. It was a little fuller in body (although still no more than medium), and had more prominent fruit on the palate. Less sour cherry and more black cherry coming to the fore, by comparison. The tannins were well rounded and the acidity was lurking in the background, keeping the fruit in check and leading to a clean finish. I enjoyed this a lot, and it reminded me and my dad of our fun visit with the winemaker, Bobby Moy, back in September. If you haven’t tried his wines yet, do yourself a favor and check them out.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
Some stuff out of my normal comfort zone today.

2016 Digioia-Royer Chambolle-Musigny Les Fremières Vieilles Vignes

Quite dark, only slightly translucent garnet with slight age on the rim. Nose is medium intensity with tart cherries, cranberries, lots of clean red fruit, but with a very nice backbone of savory thyme, wet earth, touch of tar and smoke, mushrooms and some crushed black pepper. Palate is medium to medium+ bodied with earthy minerality, some cherries and strawberries. Finish is long, not too complex but with very pleasant grippy tannins. Likely to improve over a few more years, but already drinks well. Good stuff!

2014 R. López de Heredia Rioja Blanco Viña Gravonia

My first LdH white, I usually drink Chablis or Sancerre so bear that in mind when considering my note.
Slightly developed golden/straw. Oxidized and developed nose with vanilla, slight lemon/citrus, some savory herbaceous notes, lots of dill, tree bark, wet moss and soil, and mushrooms. Palate is somewhat fresh and clean initially with lemon and citrus peel, lots of green herbs, but the secondary/tertiary notes of mushrooms and wet forest floor take over. Somehow all this stays in control and just makes this a very interesting, complex and enjoyable wine. I have no clue if this wine will develop or improve. It seems like it’s a bottle of controlled chaos right now, but I like it.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked:
'90 Beaucastel is a risky proposition. I have had bottles muddled with VA, Brett, and TCA as well as poorly stored bottles that were either dead or cooked. But I have also had many great bottles so I keep going back to the well. I opened a bottle last night that I purchased at auction this past summer. The bottle had a good fill and no signs of seepage when I cut the foil. The cork was soaked 3/4 of the way through and by the time I got the cork halfway out, I could smell the TCA. This was one of those strange situations where it was more corked on the nose than the palate. In fact the palate showed a lot of nice fruit, spice and acidity. I actually drank an enjoyable glass while holding my nose. However at that point the palate started to fade, so I switched to bubbles.

2016 William & Mary Shifflett Ranch Cabernet Sauvignon

image1920×2560 414 KB

Decanted for 3.5 hours and consumed over the next two. Velvety deep magenta in the glass. Opens with an intriguing bouquet of spring garden, crushed wild berries, and a pinch of brown sugar. Super ouillé (non oxidative) Savagnin. No funk or excessive VA here. Just a great complex wine filled with mineral salty notes and lemon. High acidity, full body. posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Julien Pilon - Lône - Condrieu 2021

Fresh aromas of hay, lemon and white peach, very enticing, but the taste is something else altogether: a bewitchingly complex combination of light honey, lemon and especially mirabelle, with a strong note of hazelnut and a little pear on the finish. Quite fresh and sappy, with none of the lushness I’ve found in most Condrieus, this is a wine to sip and contemplate. Not cheap at 40 euros but worth every penny.

I’m not normally a fan of wines from the Rhône valley, not because I don’t like them, but because they go straight to my head - why, I have no idea. Anyway, I discovered this thanks to this year’s Advent calendar and will certainly go back for more.

2015 Domaine de Chevalier

From an OWC 6-pack. Youthful deep garnet with just a touch of age on the rim. Nose is beautiful and complex with dark fruit, cassis, savory herbs, coffee, tobacco, touch of menthol, starting to show evolved notes with some dried kirsch and figs. Palate is full bodied with lift from decent acidity. It is somewhat fruit driven with cassis, slightly tart red fruit, but still clearly in Old World with earthy tones, lots of cedarwood, dark chocolate and coffee. Finishes long, complex with grippy tannins. Very good stuff, I do prefer the younger vintages slightly over this, but this is still probably at least a decade from hitting prime time as it only has very slight hints of secondary development.

posts.name: Mikko Tuomi
posts.cooked:
I brought this as part of a bigger tasting, I’ll just drop this one note because I wanted to get it off my CT.
My sixth sense tells me eventually we’ll get a much more detailed TN from this one…

2019 Sine Qua Non Syrah Distenta I

Intense red/purple color. Nose is full of ripe fruit, blueberries, crushed pepper, slight alcohol lift. Palate is ripe, clean and has a beautiful purity in the red/blue fruit/berries, with nice finish of spices, and soft tannins. Obviously has high alcohol but is well integrated and does not really jump out, just provides some nice warmth in the end. Very approachable now but very, very primary. Not much acidity or structure, this is mostly about the beauty and purity of the fruit. It has oak but it’s not prominent. You can tell they are working with some A++ material with their fruit, but perhaps this lacks some character? Is it a beautiful bottle, heck yes. Is it worth the hype, price and a 20 year wait? Absolutely not.

Burgundy red with touch of developed mahogany hints. Nice open clean and fruity nose with red tart fruit, very ripe cranberries, cherries, some licorice root, and forest floor. Palate has good acidity, tart cranberries, more cherries and some raspberry notes. Finishes short and simple with somewhat grainy but resolved tannins. I bet this was a tannic monster when it was young. Nice wine, seems to drink fine but isn’t showing any decline yet, so likely good for at least another 5 years. Not sure if it will improve though, I think this is what the wine wants to be.

… after fighting with the d%@n wax capsule and saying lots of nasty words, cursing the whole country of Sweden (where Adam is from) and secretly hoping Adam hits his toe on a bed post in the morning …

Dark youthful purple color, nose has quite a bit of VA, not very fruity, lots of mushrooms, nutmeg, moss, forest floor and tree bark. Interestingly, palate is the opposite, it is fresh, has some acidity, medium to full bodied, nice array of ripe blue fruits, slight jammy notes but not annoyingly so, and a touch of vanilla. Finishes with light tannins and very nice pure blueberry compote. This was pop n pour so the VA might blow out after some time, but I love the pure blueberry finish in this wine.

Have been trying bargain Pinots.

Trader Joe’s Grand Reserve Pinot Noir 2021 San Luis Obispo County Lot #112

Incredibly intense but rounded red fruit flavor from the start. Tart cherry with maybe a hint of cranberry (or even blood-orange) is the primary flavor, with some cinnamon and baking spices mid-palate. Clean finish and it remained delicious throughout the whole bottle. Not super-complex, but for my tastes this is the exact kind of flavor that I like in cheaper Pinots. Outstanding QPR at $12.99. Really wonder where this is from since I want to buy more bottles of it.

Costco Kirkland Pinot Noir Russian River Valley 2021

Woody, lots of tannins, some good cherry and cherry-cola flavor, but feels like I’m chewing wood residue at first. Got better as it opened up and the second day the tannins were less pronounced and the flavor was better-integrated, but I was overall underwhelmed, especially having this alongside the TJ’s one. Not something I would pick up again, even at $12.99.

2016 Bougetz Cellars Cabernet Sauvignon Ardante

Purple/ruby. Initially lots of brett with band aid on pop and pour. Decanted 24 hours. Nose with cassis, dark cherries, oak, alcohol, chocolate.
Full bodied palate with spices, dark cassis and currants, touch of tart underripe strawberries, some tobacco, finishes long and a little tart with nice good quality tannins. I was surprised with the amount of brett it had with very strong band air that takes 24+ hours to blow off, and even then is still sort of hangs in there. posts.name: John Maguire
posts.cooked:
image2940×2726 1.88 MB

Chateau Cheval Blanc 1990

(Note from Cellar Tracker)

1.5 hour decant. Last bottle and the cosmos saved me the best for last. The forensic list of smells and tastes are similar to my last encounter: cassis/raspberry compote/blackberry, tobacco leaf, burning peat, roses, leather, grass, and on and on. However this time everything was more sharply delineated and focused - hence more specific notes this time. Tannins are still very strong giving the the wine a formidable backbone. At the same time the wine goes down like liquid silk - an amazing contrast of smoothness and structure I encounter rarely. Balance is just - impeccable. This is why we drink claret. Why only 99? Two reasons: 1. I have actually had a better Cheval Blanc (1953). 2. After a lot of time in the glass - oh yes, you want to enjoy this slowly - eventually a slight bitter edge creeps into the finish. But this is fricken quibbling and probably a reflection of my crappy-ass decanting rather than the wine.
This bottle was at its peak. This is a seriously outperforming 1990. Wow.
Score: 99. Relative to expectations: +++

Bienaime setup his Jura estate a few years back after working a decade at Laval in Champagne.

My favorite wines from a dinner featuring 12 Cali (mostly) Pinots:

2004 Calera Pinot Noir ‘Selleck Vineyard’
Stood out for being particularly forward and accessible, with plenty of sweet reddish fruit - though verging on jammy - and a distinct sarsaparilla note; some complexity to the flavors too; this is C Major; good plus

2011 Chanin Pinot Noir ‘Los Alamos Vineyard’
Dark and so stemmy that it’s a little unpleasant at first, though that lightens up with air; layered and lively fruit with a real driving acidity to it; similar in expression to its neighbor (the 2016 Young Hagen Riddle Vineyard) but with an extra sparkle, and I gave the edge to the Chanin in the end; my spouse’s favorite; good plus

2016 Au Bon Climat Pinot Noir ‘Isabelle’
A serene, monolithic black hole of a wine; my reference point for this is the 2005 Roumier Bonnes Mares; it does seem a tad low on acidity but not distractingly so; who knows what this will become in the future?; good with upside

2014 Les Héritiers du Comte Lafon Saint Véran

Slightly developed straw color. Nice nose with lots of minerals, flint, citrus, stone fruits, some leesy/yeasty richness and a touch of oak/spice like crushed white pepper. Palate is youthful, rich and has freshness from medium acidity. Lots of lemon, slight buttery/spicy oaky tones in the background, but in good balance now. Finishes somewhat long and pleasant with lots of minerality. A very nice wine that drinks much younger than I expected. It is not the most complex wine in the world, but it is clearly very well made. This would easily keep and maybe even slightly improve for another 10 years.

2019 Ser Cabernet Pfeffer Young Vine Enz Vineyard

Very pinot-like color, maybe just slightly inkier tone. Somewhat simple fruity nose with dark cherries, strawberries, pomegranate, crushed pepper and a slight hint of balsamic VA. Palate is light bodied, juicy, fresh, low+ acidity, very slightly carbonic and very fruity with cranberries, tart strawberries, some cloves and peppercorns. Finishes somewhat short and simple with very light tannins and some spicy tones.
Nice wine, it’s not the most serious or complex wine, but I doubt it tries to be one either.

This was a BD14 buy for me, and heck, I might buy again next year if given the opportunity. Nice easy Tuesday wine.

posts.cooked:
Little tasting with my local caviste yesterday:

IMG-20230309-WA00001920×1080 260 KB

The Mailly is a decent enough Champagne (I think 70% PN and 30% Chard) but it isn’t very exciting nor a particularly good QPR.

The following bottle was the Pinot Beurrot which I think I already described here.

Then followed the Pommard which was wonderful albeit on the young side. Nice nose of black and red berries and hints of spices. Nicely concentrated, plenty of freshness and some powdery tannins with some grip. Good length. I bought some of it.

The Marsannay was also enjoyable but lacked from following the Pommard. It’s red fruited and lively with good acidity. The pairing was ambitious with a Munster espuma which essentially made the tannins disappear.

The star of the show came out with the éclair au caramel: Château de Meursault Marc de Bourgogne 2008. It was served lightly chilled which made it easier to palate for some. The aromas really came out as it warmed up and this is an explosion of fresh grapes, dried fruits, toasted almonds and wood spices. This is absolutely wonderful. I bought a bottle instantly. Only made in good vintages in very small quantity, they get the grapes from their parcels in Pommard Clos des Epenots, Beaune Grèves, Beaune Fèves, Volnay Clos des Chênes and Corton.

Visited my caviste again today to try some of his new arrivals from Domaine de Montine. The Côtes-du-Rhône (Syrah, Grenache, Cinsault) is a decent wine but the initial nose was reduced and rather green (guarrigue). It lacks length and concentration on the mid-palate. Their Secret de Terroir cuvée is better (50% Syrah, 50% Grenache): black fruits, good acidity some green elements on the mid-palate decent length. Their Rosé Muse i was not a fan of: the nose is nice enough with plenty of fruit but it’s a little dilute on the palate. Very little to like.

I had brought a 2018 Mullineux Syrah and it did not impress either. A lot of tannins but lacking in fruit even though the nose shows a good dose of black fruits. We finished with a Turkish red, can’t remember the name, that was even less of a pleasure to drink.

The short answer is terrific.

Had my miss taste it blind, except liking it though thinking it would benefit from a bit more aging I had her guess the vintage, “2020?”

I can’t blame her though because it was such a fresh wine, not even the colors showed signs of aging (watery rim, red garnet).

Wish I tasted it in its youth to have a reference (drank my 1/3 bottles about 3 years ago, also lovely) seems it reached a plateau and just keeps going.

Taste-wise: Cherries, raspberries, wild strawberries. Menthol, herbs, licorice, Asian spices. Tannins are silky yet a dusty graveliness to it. A long sapidic finish.

The silkiness of the tannins and perhaps a hint of leather together with the spices were the main things giving away that it’s a 25 year old wine.

You are in for a treat (wish I had that as well) and I don’t think you need to to wait nowhere near as long till they reach the peak and reach the plateau.

posts.name: Jim Stewart
posts.cooked:
I have learned to look at things, wine and otherwise, that a true “wow” experience is a gift. It really cannot be planned or predicted, although some of us certainly try to do that. We look for the potential “wow”, plot a course for it, and set sail. Often though, we find it unexpectedly.

Tonight I was gifted a “wow” pairing of a Chianti with pizza. Enjoyable at first without food, with a restrained brightness, then with the pizza (slices of pepperoni, cheese, and garlic) the wine went “Mama Mia!”. Tasty and complex fruit, herbs, and earth. Memory is not my strong point, but I will call this my best wine and pizza pairing ever. Wow!

Rocca delle Macie Chianti Classico Riserva 2018

Still youthful dark ruby with touch of purple color. Very long viscous legs. Nose is somewhat intense with a textbook Bordeaux profile of cedarwood, leather, tobacco, cassis and red fruits. Palate is full- bodied, fresh, young, chewy with nice touch of acidity. Ripe cranberries, blackberries, touch of forest floor and cedar. Finishes somewhat long with tannins that are just about to start resolving and integrating better.
This is young but about to enter it’s drinking window. Very nice wine but likely better 2-3 years from now, and best perhaps in a decade.

Light golden/yellow color. Nose is medium intensity with ripe yellow fruit, minerals, salinity, diacetyl, lemon pith, and some savoriness. Palate is creamy with nice lift from balanced acidity. Lemon/citrus, minerals, salt, some tropical fruit, and interesting touch of nuttiness. Finishes long, somewhat complex with more saltiness and toasted nuts.

posts.name: Jon Lawrence
posts.cooked: I used to like CDP and I thought '07 was a great vintage when I tasted it on release. I bought Beaucastel, Pegau, Usseglio, Janasse and Clos de Pape among others thinking this was going to be the next '90. But over the years I have become very sensitive to brett and high alcohol wines. Well a lot of these wines turned into hot, extracted, stinky messes and I sold off everything except for Beaucastel and small portion of the Pegau. I was thinking maybe it is time to finally clean house of these wines so I decided to pull the cork on a '07 Beaucastel recently. I drank a glass one night and then we finished the bottle a couple of nights later. I was shocked at how good it was. But for the pepper and herbaceous components, it drank like a really good Bordeaux. It has acidity, no sign of heat, brett or kirsch. I would drink this with a steak any day. I am going to keep my Beaucastel. I am less hopeful about the Pegau after reading through the CT notes. Seems as if it is as funky as ever.

  • 2020 Beau Rivage Chenin Blanc - USA, California, Central Valley, Clarksburg (3/29/2023)
    Woof. This is a sexy beast of a Chenin, if ultra-precise, laser-cut, and pulverized rocks dialed up to 11 are your thing. Very young and primary, almost painfully so, with electric acid (just shy of eye-watering) on the attack that is reminiscent of the best Muscadets (here I think about '14 Pepiere Briords), a frontal assault of seaspray, minerals (chalk), some citrus pith and then more minerals (granite). Initially, it's so tightly coiled that the only chenin-typicity it shows are whispers of a hard-ass Savennieres maybe. Over the course of 4 hours, woolly notes and honey emerge from the chiseled structure - oh that's nice. Not for those afraid of un-fruity wines. Boy this is good and perhaps will turn into something magical in another 5-10 years. Most similar to the 2017 (ie. better than 18 and 19), but with even more precision and depth. 93+ (93 points)

2020 Domaine Louis Michel Chablis - France, Burgundy, Chablis (3/31/2023)

The reduction blew off the glass in mere seconds. Ripe peach, melon initially but then the seashells, iodine whiff comes in nicely. Super enjoyable, drinks beautifully right out of the gate.
